questions.stem: A particle moves along the $x$-axis with position $x(t) = 3t^2 - 12t + 7$, in metres with $t$ in seconds. What is its velocity at $t = 1\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: Velocity is the derivative of position: $v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = 6t - 12$. At $t = 1\ \text{s}$ this gives $v = 6 - 12 = -6\ \text{m/s}$, so the particle is moving in the negative direction. The trap is $-2\ \text{m/s}$, which substitutes $t = 1$ into the position $x(1) = 3 - 12 + 7 = -2$ and reports a position as a velocity.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle moves along a straight line with position $x(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + 2$, in metres with $t$ in seconds. What is its acceleration at $t = 3\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: Differentiate twice. The velocity is $v(t) = 3t^2 - 12t + 9$ and the acceleration is $a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = 6t - 12$. At $t = 3\ \text{s}$, $a = 18 - 12 = 6\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $0\ \text{m/s}^2$, which is the value of the velocity at $t = 3\ \text{s}$, where the particle is momentarily at rest but is still accelerating.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle starts from rest and moves with acceleration $a(t) = 6t\ \text{m/s}^2$ along a straight line. What is its velocity at $t = 2\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: When the acceleration depends on time, integrate it to get the velocity: $v(t) = v_0 + \int_0^t 6t'\,dt' = 0 + 3t^2$. At $t = 2\ \text{s}$, $v = 3(4) = 12\ \text{m/s}$. The constant-acceleration formula $v = at$ does not apply here because $a$ is not constant. The trap is $24\ \text{m/s}$, which wrongly uses $v = a(2)\cdot t = 6(2)(2)$ as if the acceleration were the constant $12\ \text{m/s}^2$ throughout.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A car accelerates uniformly from $10\ \text{m/s}$ to $30\ \text{m/s}$ in $5\ \text{s}$. What distance does it cover during this interval?
questions.explanation: For uniform acceleration the distance equals the average velocity times the time. The average velocity is $\tfrac{1}{2}(u + v) = \tfrac{1}{2}(10 + 30) = 20\ \text{m/s}$, so the distance is $20 \times 5 = 100\ \text{m}$. Equivalently, $a = (30 - 10)/5 = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$ and $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 = 50 + 50 = 100\ \text{m}$. The trap is $150\ \text{m}$, which multiplies the final speed $30\ \text{m/s}$ by the time, ignoring that the car was slower for most of the interval.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A car travelling at $25\ \text{m/s}$ brakes with uniform deceleration and stops in $50\ \text{m}$. What is the magnitude of the deceleration?
questions.explanation: Use $v^2 = u^2 - 2as$ with $v = 0$, $u = 25\ \text{m/s}$, $s = 50\ \text{m}$. Then $0 = 625 - 2a(50)$, so $100a = 625$ and $a = 6.25\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $12.5\ \text{m/s}^2$, which computes $u^2/s = 625/50$ and forgets the factor of $2$ that multiplies $as$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A ball is dropped from rest from a height of $45\ \text{m}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. With what speed does it hit the ground?
questions.explanation: With $u = 0$, $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh = 2(10)(45) = 900$, so $v = 30\ \text{m/s}$. As a check, the fall takes $t = \sqrt{2h/g} = \sqrt{9} = 3\ \text{s}$, and $v = gt = 30\ \text{m/s}$ confirms it. The trap is $90\ \text{m/s}$, which takes $v^2 = 900$ and reports $900/10$, mixing up the squared speed with the speed itself.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A stone is thrown vertically upward from ground level at $30\ \text{m/s}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. At what times is the stone at a height of $40\ \text{m}$ above the ground?
questions.explanation: Take up as positive: $y = 30t - 5t^2$. Setting $y = 40$ gives $5t^2 - 30t + 40 = 0$, that is $t^2 - 6t + 8 = 0$, which factors as $(t - 2)(t - 4) = 0$. So the stone passes $40\ \text{m}$ at $t = 2\ \text{s}$ going up and again at $t = 4\ \text{s}$ coming down. The trap is $t = 2\ \text{s}$ only, which keeps just the upward crossing and forgets that the stone returns through the same height on the way down.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A ball is thrown upward at $20\ \text{m/s}$ from the edge of a cliff and lands on the ground $60\ \text{m}$ below the launch point. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. How long is the ball in the air?
questions.explanation: Measure displacement from the launch point with up positive, so landing is at $y = -60\ \text{m}$. Then $-60 = 20t - 5t^2$, which rearranges to $t^2 - 4t - 12 = 0$ and factors as $(t - 6)(t + 2) = 0$. The physical root is $t = 6\ \text{s}$; the negative root is discarded. The trap is $4\ \text{s}$, the time $2u/g$ to return to the launch height, which ignores the extra $60\ \text{m}$ of fall below the cliff.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A projectile is launched from level ground at $40\ \text{m/s}$ at $30^\circ$ above the horizontal. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. What is its horizontal range?
questions.explanation: The range on level ground is $R = u^2\sin 2\theta/g$, with twice the launch angle entering the sine. Here $\sin 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2$, so $R = 1600(\sqrt{3}/2)/10 = 80\sqrt{3} \approx 138.6\ \text{m}$. As a check, the time of flight is $2u\sin 30^\circ/g = 2(20)/10 = 4\ \text{s}$ and the horizontal speed is $40\cos 30^\circ = 20\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$, giving $4 \times 20\sqrt{3} = 80\sqrt{3}\ \text{m}$. The trap is $40\sqrt{3}\ \text{m}$, which uses $\sin\theta$ instead of $\sin 2\theta$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A ball is thrown horizontally at $15\ \text{m/s}$ from the top of a tower $20\ \text{m}$ high. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. How far from the base of the tower does it land?
questions.explanation: The vertical and horizontal motions are independent. The time to fall is found from $20 = \tfrac{1}{2}(10)t^2$, so $t^2 = 4$ and $t = 2\ \text{s}$. During that time the horizontal distance is $15 \times 2 = 30\ \text{m}$. The trap is $15\ \text{m}$, which uses $t = 1\ \text{s}$, the value that would come from $20 = 10t^2$ if the factor of one half were dropped from the fall equation.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A projectile launched from level ground has a horizontal velocity component of $15\ \text{m/s}$ and stays in the air for $4\ \text{s}$ before returning to the ground. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What was its launch speed?
questions.explanation: The time of flight fixes the vertical component through $T = 2u_y/g$, so $u_y = gT/2 = 10(4)/2 = 20\ \text{m/s}$. The launch speed is the magnitude of the full launch velocity, $u = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2} = \sqrt{15^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{625} = 25\ \text{m/s}$, a 3-4-5 triangle. The trap is $35\ \text{m/s}$, which adds the two components $15 + 20$ directly instead of combining them in quadrature.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle moves in a circle of radius $2\ \text{m}$ at a constant speed of $6\ \text{m/s}$. What is the magnitude of its acceleration?
questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion the speed is constant but the velocity direction is not, so there is a centripetal acceleration directed toward the centre with magnitude $a = v^2/r = 36/2 = 18\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $0\ \text{m/s}^2$, which reasons that constant speed means no acceleration; the direction of the velocity is changing, so the acceleration is nonzero.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A stone is whirled in a horizontal circle of radius $0.5\ \text{m}$, completing $2$ revolutions every second. What is the magnitude of its centripetal acceleration? Use $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$.
questions.explanation: The angular speed is $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi(2) = 4\pi\ \text{rad/s}$. The centripetal acceleration is $a = \omega^2 r = (4\pi)^2(0.5) = 16\pi^2(0.5) = 8\pi^2 \approx 8(9.87) \approx 79\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $\approx 39.5\ \text{m/s}^2$, which uses $\omega = 2\pi f$ but then computes $a = \tfrac{1}{2}\omega^2 r$ or equivalently halves the radius twice, dropping a factor of $2$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A car drives around a circular track of radius $50\ \text{m}$. At one instant its speed is $20\ \text{m/s}$ and it is speeding up at a rate of $6\ \text{m/s}^2$ along the track. What is the magnitude of its total acceleration at that instant?
questions.explanation: In non-uniform circular motion the acceleration has a tangential part $a_t = 6\ \text{m/s}^2$ (the rate of change of speed) and a radial part $a_r = v^2/r = 400/50 = 8\ \text{m/s}^2$ (directed to the centre). These are perpendicular, so the total magnitude is $\sqrt{a_t^2 + a_r^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $8\ \text{m/s}^2$, the radial part alone, which forgets that the car is also changing speed.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A wheel of radius $0.3\ \text{m}$ rotates at $300$ revolutions per minute. What is the linear speed of a point on its rim?
questions.explanation: Convert the rotation rate to radians per second: $300\ \text{rev/min} = 300/60 = 5\ \text{rev/s}$, so $\omega = 2\pi(5) = 10\pi\ \text{rad/s}$. The rim speed is $v = \omega r = 10\pi(0.3) = 3\pi \approx 9.4\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $1.5\pi\ \text{m/s}$, which uses $\omega = \pi f$ instead of $2\pi f$ and so drops a factor of $2$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Rain falls vertically at $5\ \text{m/s}$ relative to the ground. A cyclist rides on a straight horizontal road at $5\ \text{m/s}$. At what speed does the rain strike the cyclist, in the cyclist's frame?
questions.explanation: In the cyclist's frame the rain has the ground velocity of the rain minus the cyclist's velocity. The rain's velocity is $5\ \text{m/s}$ downward and the cyclist's is $5\ \text{m/s}$ horizontal, so relative to the cyclist the rain has a $5\ \text{m/s}$ downward component and a $5\ \text{m/s}$ backward component. These are perpendicular, so the speed is $\sqrt{5^2 + 5^2} = 5\sqrt{2} \approx 7.1\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $5\ \text{m/s}$, which keeps only the vertical fall speed and ignores the apparent horizontal motion of the rain.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A swimmer who can swim at $1.5\ \text{m/s}$ in still water heads straight across a river whose current flows at $2\ \text{m/s}$. What is the swimmer's speed relative to the bank?
questions.explanation: The swimmer's velocity relative to the bank is the vector sum of the swim velocity (across, $1.5\ \text{m/s}$) and the current velocity (downstream, $2\ \text{m/s}$). These are perpendicular, so the resultant speed is $\sqrt{1.5^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 4} = \sqrt{6.25} = 2.5\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $3.5\ \text{m/s}$, which adds the two speeds directly as if they pointed along the same line.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two cars travel along the same straight road in the same direction, one at $30\ \text{m/s}$ and the other at $22\ \text{m/s}$, with the faster car $100\ \text{m}$ behind the slower one. How long does the faster car take to catch up?
questions.explanation: Work in the slower car's frame. For motion in the same direction the relative speed is the difference $30 - 22 = 8\ \text{m/s}$, and the gap to close is $100\ \text{m}$, so the time is $100/8 = 12.5\ \text{s}$. The trap is $\approx 1.9\ \text{s}$, which divides by the sum $30 + 22 = 52\ \text{m/s}$, treating the cars as approaching head-on.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle moves in the plane with velocity $\mathbf{v}(t) = (4t)\,\hat{\imath} + (3)\,\hat{\jmath}$, in m/s with $t$ in seconds, starting from the origin. What is its position vector at $t = 2\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: Integrate each component from $0$ to $t$. The $x$-position is $\int_0^t 4t'\,dt' = 2t^2$, and the $y$-position is $\int_0^t 3\,dt' = 3t$. At $t = 2\ \text{s}$ this gives $x = 2(4) = 8\ \text{m}$ and $y = 3(2) = 6\ \text{m}$, so $\mathbf{r} = (8\hat{\imath} + 6\hat{\jmath})\ \text{m}$. The trap is $(16\hat{\imath} + 6\hat{\jmath})\ \text{m}$, which integrates the $x$-velocity as $4t^2$ and drops the one half from $\int 4t\,dt = 2t^2$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle moves so that $\mathbf{r}(t) = (R\cos\omega t)\,\hat{\imath} + (R\sin\omega t)\,\hat{\jmath}$ with constant $R$ and $\omega$. Which statement about its velocity and acceleration is correct?
options: Both the velocity and the acceleration are tangent to the circle.
The velocity is tangent to the circle and the acceleration points toward the centre.
The velocity points toward the centre and the acceleration is tangent to the circle.
Both the velocity and the acceleration point toward the centre.
questions.explanation: Differentiating, $\mathbf{v} = R\omega(-\sin\omega t\,\hat{\imath} + \cos\omega t\,\hat{\jmath})$, which is perpendicular to $\mathbf{r}$ (their dot product is zero), so the velocity is tangent to the circle. Differentiating again, $\mathbf{a} = -R\omega^2(\cos\omega t\,\hat{\imath} + \sin\omega t\,\hat{\jmath}) = -\omega^2\mathbf{r}$, which points opposite to $\mathbf{r}$, that is, toward the centre. This is the hallmark of uniform circular motion. The trap is the last option, which forgets that the centripetal acceleration must point inward, not along the direction of travel.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two stones are dropped from rest from the same height, the second one $1\ \text{s}$ after the first. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the separation between them $2\ \text{s}$ after the first stone is released?
questions.explanation: At $t = 2\ \text{s}$ the first stone has fallen $\tfrac{1}{2}(10)(2)^2 = 20\ \text{m}$. The second stone has been falling for only $1\ \text{s}$, so it has fallen $\tfrac{1}{2}(10)(1)^2 = 5\ \text{m}$. The separation is $20 - 5 = 15\ \text{m}$. The trap is $5\ \text{m}$, which reports the distance fallen by the second stone instead of the gap between the two.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A ball is projected at $50\ \text{m/s}$ at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal with $\sin\theta = 0.8$ and $\cos\theta = 0.6$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the speed of the ball $1\ \text{s}$ after launch?
questions.explanation: The horizontal component is untouched by gravity, $v_x = 50(0.6) = 30\ \text{m/s}$. The vertical component starts at $50(0.8) = 40\ \text{m/s}$ and after $1\ \text{s}$ is $v_y = 40 - 10(1) = 30\ \text{m/s}$. The speed is $\sqrt{30^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{1800} = 30\sqrt{2} \approx 42.4\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $50\ \text{m/s}$, which assumes the speed is unchanged from launch; gravity has reduced the vertical component, so the speed has dropped.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A boat that travels at $5\ \text{m/s}$ in still water must cross a river $120\ \text{m}$ wide whose current runs at $3\ \text{m/s}$. The pilot points the boat directly across (perpendicular to the banks). How long does the crossing take?
questions.explanation: The time to cross depends only on the component of velocity across the river, which is the $5\ \text{m/s}$ the boat makes in still water (the current runs parallel to the banks and does not help the boat cross). So the crossing time is $120/5 = 24\ \text{s}$. The current carries the boat $3 \times 24 = 72\ \text{m}$ downstream but does not change how long the crossing takes. The trap is $15\ \text{s}$, which divides the width by $8\ \text{m/s}$, the arithmetic sum of the two speeds, even though only the cross-stream component sets the time.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: An object is thrown vertically upward and reaches its maximum height $H$. Ignoring air resistance, at what fraction of $H$ is its speed equal to half its launch speed?
questions.explanation: Use $v^2 = u^2 - 2gy$. When $v = u/2$, $u^2/4 = u^2 - 2gy$, so $2gy = \tfrac{3}{4}u^2$ and $y = 3u^2/(8g)$. Since $H = u^2/(2g) = 4u^2/(8g)$, the ratio is $y/H = 3/4$. The speed has dropped to half, but the height is already three quarters of the way up because the object decelerates and spends more time high up. The trap is $\tfrac{1}{4}H$, which assumes height scales linearly with speed rather than with speed squared.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle has constant acceleration. It is at $x = 0$ with velocity $+8\ \text{m/s}$ at $t = 0$, and at $x = 0$ again at $t = 4\ \text{s}$. What is its acceleration?
questions.explanation: Use $x = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ with $x = 0$ at $t = 4\ \text{s}$: $0 = 8(4) + \tfrac{1}{2}a(16) = 32 + 8a$, so $a = -4\ \text{m/s}^2$. The particle moves out and returns to the origin, which requires a deceleration that reverses it. The trap is $-2\ \text{m/s}^2$, which sets $0 = 8 + \tfrac{1}{2}a(4)$, mistakenly using $t = 2\ \text{s}$ (the turning point) in place of the return time.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A car accelerates uniformly from rest at $3\ \text{m/s}^2$ along a straight road. Find its speed and the distance covered after $8\ \text{s}$.
questions.solution: With $u = 0$, the speed is $v = u + at = 0 + 3 \times 8 = $ {{1}} metres per second. The distance is $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 8^2 = $ {{2}} metres. The quantity that depends on the square of the time is the {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The velocity is $v = 24\ \text{m/s}$, linear in $t$. The distance is $s = \tfrac{1}{2}(3)(64) = 96\ \text{m}$, which carries the $t^2$ dependence. Reporting $96$ as a speed would confuse the two.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A ball is dropped from rest from a height of $80\ \text{m}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. Find the time to reach the ground and the impact speed.
questions.solution: From $h = \tfrac{1}{2}gt^2$, the time is $t = \sqrt{2h/g} = \sqrt{16} = $ {{1}} seconds. The impact speed is $v = gt = 10 \times t = $ {{2}} metres per second.
questions.explanation: Here $2h/g = 160/10 = 16$, so $t = 4\ \text{s}$ and $v = 10(4) = 40\ \text{m/s}$. A cross-check: $v^2 = 2gh = 1600$, so $v = 40\ \text{m/s}$ again.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A projectile is launched from level ground at $20\ \text{m/s}$ at $30^\circ$ above the horizontal. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. Find the time of flight and the horizontal range.
questions.solution: The vertical component is $u\sin 30^\circ = 20 \times 0.5 = 10\ \text{m/s}$, so the time of flight is $T = 2u\sin\theta/g = 20/10 = $ {{1}} seconds. With horizontal component $u\cos 30^\circ = 10\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$, the range is $R = u\cos\theta \cdot T \approx $ {{2}} metres.
questions.explanation: The time of flight is $2(10)/10 = 2\ \text{s}$. The horizontal speed is $20\cos 30^\circ = 10\sqrt{3} \approx 17.3\ \text{m/s}$, so $R = 17.3 \times 2 \approx 34.6\ \text{m}$, equal to $u^2\sin 60^\circ/g = 400(\sqrt{3}/2)/10 = 20\sqrt{3}\ \text{m}$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle moves in a circle of radius $4\ \text{m}$ at a constant speed of $8\ \text{m/s}$. Find its angular speed and the magnitude of its centripetal acceleration.
questions.solution: The angular speed is $\omega = v/r = 8/4 = $ {{1}} radians per second. The centripetal acceleration is $a = v^2/r = 64/4 = $ {{2}} metres per second squared, directed toward the {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Here $\omega = 2\ \text{rad/s}$ and $a = 64/4 = 16\ \text{m/s}^2$, which also equals $\omega^2 r = 4 \times 4 = 16\ \text{m/s}^2$. The centripetal acceleration always points toward the centre of the circle.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle moves along the $x$-axis with position $x(t) = 2t^3 - 9t^2 + 12t$, in metres with $t$ in seconds, for $t \ge 0$. Find the times at which it is momentarily at rest and its acceleration at $t = 0$.
questions.solution: The velocity is $v(t) = 6t^2 - 18t + 12 = 6(t-1)(t-2)$, which is zero at $t = $ {{1}} second and at $t = $ {{2}} seconds. The acceleration is $a(t) = 12t - 18$, so at $t = 0$ it equals {{3}} metres per second squared.
questions.explanation: Setting $v = 6(t-1)(t-2) = 0$ gives $t = 1\ \text{s}$ and $t = 2\ \text{s}$. Differentiating once more, $a(0) = 12(0) - 18 = -18\ \text{m/s}^2$. Being at rest means $v = 0$, not $a = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A swimmer who swims at $1.5\ \text{m/s}$ in still water heads straight across a river whose current is $2\ \text{m/s}$. Find the swimmer's speed relative to the bank, and state which way the resultant points compared with straight across.
questions.solution: The cross-stream and downstream velocities are perpendicular, so the speed relative to the bank is $\sqrt{1.5^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{6.25} = $ {{1}} metres per second. Compared with the straight-across direction, the resultant path is carried {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The resultant speed is $\sqrt{2.25 + 4} = \sqrt{6.25} = 2.5\ \text{m/s}$. Because the swimmer points straight across while the current pushes along the bank, the actual path is angled downstream.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics
